messages.content: hi
Hello! How can I assist you today?
can you help me /
Of course! Let me know what you need help with, and I'll do my best to assist you.

Which feature lies between the spine and posterior body of the scapula?

a. Supraspinous fossa

b. Suprascapular notch

c. Infraspinous fossa

d. Glenoid cavity

The feature that lies between the spine and the posterior body of the scapula is:

c. Infraspinous fossa

The infraspinous fossa is the broad surface on the posterior (back) side of the scapula, below the scapular spine. It serves as the origin for the infraspinatus muscle.

Which humerus bone land mark articulate with head of radius?

a. Capitulum

b. Lateral epicondyle

c. Trochlea

 d. Radial fossa

a. Capitulum

The capitulum of the humerus articulates with the head of the radius. It is a rounded eminence at the distal end of the humerus, which provides a joint surface for the proximal radius, allowing for the hinge movement of the elbow and the rotational movement of the forearm.

The radial notch of ulna is the articulation that form the distal radioulnar joint?

a. False

b. True

a. False

The radial notch of the ulna is where it articulates with the head of the radius to form the proximal radioulnar joint, not the distal radioulnar joint. The distal radioulnar joint is formed by the head of the ulna and the ulnar notch on the distal end of the radius.

Long, slender; not split; points inferiorly. What spine region this spinous process?

a. Cervical vertebrae

b. Thoracic vertebrae

c. Lumbar vertebra

d. Sacrum vertebrae

a. Cervical vertebrae

The spinous processes of the cervical vertebrae are typically long, slender, and often bifid (split into two parts), but not always clearly split, and they point inferiorly. The description provided doesn't clearly state "split," which might lead to some confusion, but the other options (thoracic, lumbar, and sacrum) do not generally have long and slender spinous processes that point directly inferiorly. Thoracic vertebrae spinous processes tend to be long and point inferiorly but are more robust, lumbar processes are shorter and stouter, and the sacrum, composed of fused vertebrae, does not have distinct spinous processes in the same way cervical, thoracic, or lumbar vertebrae do.

Which one of the following ligaments stabilize the clavicle and the corocoid process?

Time left 0:45:35

a. Coracocacromial ligament

b. Coracoclavicular ligament

c. Coracohumeral ligament

d. Acromioclacicular ligament

b. Coracoclavicular ligament

The coracoclavicular ligament connects the clavicle (collarbone) to the coracoid process of the scapula. This ligament plays a key role in stabilizing the clavicle and preventing its dislocation at the acromioclavicular (AC) joint.

Which one of the following is a depression marks the path of radial nerve?

a. Radial groove

b. Intertubercular groove

c. Medial epicondyle

d. Coronoid fossa

a. Radial groove

The radial groove, also known as the spiral groove, is a depression on the posterior surface of the humerus where the radial nerve runs. It marks the path of the radial nerve and the deep brachial artery as they travel down the arm.

Which vertebrae of the vertebral column have no spinous process?

a. L5

b. S1

C. C1

d. C2

c. C1

C1, also known as the atlas, is the first cervical vertebra and it does not have a spinous process. Instead, it has a posterior arch and an anterior arch with a tubercle where muscles and ligaments attach. The atlas is unique among the vertebrae as it supports the skull and allows for the nodding motion of the head.

Between which vertebrae of the vertebral column has no disc?

 a. C7-T1

b. C2-C3

c. L5-S1

d. C1-C2

d. C1-C2

There is no intervertebral disc between the C1 and C2 vertebrae of the cervical spine. These two vertebrae, known as the atlas (C1) and the axis (C2), form the atlantoaxial joint, which is a pivot type of joint allowing for the rotation of the head. Instead of a disc, the articulation between these two vertebrae consists of bony and ligamentous structures that permit this unique range of motion.

It connect bone to bone, flexible not elastic and prevent joint movement. What is it?



a. Ligament

b. Pivot joint

c. Uniaxial joint

d. Connective tissue

a. Ligament

Ligaments are structures that connect bone to bone. They are made of dense connective tissue that is flexible to accommodate movement, but generally not elastic, as they are not meant to stretch significantly. Ligaments help stabilize joints and limit their range of motion to prevent injury.

What spin region does have costal facets?

a. Lumbar region

 b. Sacrum region

c. Thoracic region

d. Cervical region

c. Thoracic region

The thoracic vertebrae have costal facets for articulation with the ribs. Each typical thoracic vertebra has superior and inferior costal facets on the sides of the vertebral body for articulation with the head of the ribs, and transverse costal facets on the transverse processes for articulation with the tubercle of the ribs.

What is the anterior and inferior portion of the acetabulum incomplete ridge and leaving a gap?

a. Acetabular notch

b. All answers are wrong

c. Lunate surface

d. Illium

a. Acetabular notch

The acetabular notch is a gap or indentation in the inferior part of the acetabulum, the socket in the hip bone that receives the head of the femur (thighbone). This notch is bridged by a ligament called the transverse acetabular ligament, which converts the notch into a foramen through which blood vessels and nerves pass.

Which one of the following considered as Gliding movement in a joint?

a. Between sternum and clavicle

b. Tarsal bones

c. Carbal bones

d. All answer are right

d. All answers are right

Gliding movements occur between flat surfaces of bones that slide over one another. These types of movements are typically seen in the following joints:

a. Between the sternum and clavicle (sternoclavicular joint)
b. Between tarsal bones in the foot (inter-tarsal joints)
c. Between carpal bones in the wrist (inter-carpal joints)

All of these joints allow for limited gliding movements as part of their normal range of motion.

What is the difference of female coccyx to male coccyx which points anteriorly?

a. Points superiorly

b. Points posteriorly

c. Points inferiorly

d. Points anteriorly

a. Points superiorly

In general, the female coccyx is more movable and points more posteriorly (backward), which helps to facilitate childbirth. The male coccyx is often less movable and tends to point anteriorly (forward). The difference in orientation is related to the anatomical differences in the male and female pelvis. The option "Points superiorly" reflects a misunderstanding of the question, as the coccyx orientation is usually described in terms of anterior or posterior direction rather than superior or inferior.

Which ribs do not have tubercular facets and do not contact the transverse process of thoracic vertebrae?

a. Ribs 11-12

b. Ribs 8-10

c. Ribs 10-11

d. Ribs 9-10

a. Ribs 11-12

Ribs 11 and 12 are known as floating ribs and do not have tubercular facets. They also do not articulate with the transverse processes of the thoracic vertebrae. These ribs are shorter and have a free posterior end that does not connect to the sternum or to the costal cartilage of the ribs above.

Which one of the vertebral column region the vertebral foramen small and triangular shap?



a. Thoracic

b. Cervical

c. Sacrum

d. Lumbar

a. Thoracic

In the thoracic region of the vertebral column, the vertebral foramen is typically small and triangular in shape. This contrasts with the cervical region where the foramen is large and triangular, and the lumbar region where it is large and more oval or kidney-shaped. The sacral region, being composed of fused vertebrae, forms a canal known as the sacral canal, which is a continuation of the vertebral canal.

Two __________extend between the femur and the heads of the tibia and fibula. These ligaments reinforce the knee joint's posterior surface. What are they?

a. Popliteal ligaments

 b. Posterior cruciate ligament

c. Patellar ligament

d. Anterior cruciate ligament

a. Popliteal ligaments

The two popliteal ligaments, referred to as the oblique popliteal ligament and the arcuate popliteal ligament, extend across the back of the knee joint. They help to reinforce the posterior joint capsule and stabilize the knee. The posterior cruciate ligament and anterior cruciate ligament are also critical for knee stability, but they are located inside the joint capsule rather than extending across the posterior surface like the popliteal ligaments. The patellar ligament is an extension of the quadriceps tendon that connects the patella (kneecap) to the tibia (shinbone).

Which one of the following intervertebral ligament connects thee anterior surface of adjacent vertebral bodies?

a. The anterior longitudinal ligament

b. The ligamentum flavum

c. The posterior longitudinal ligament

d. The interspinous ligament

a. The anterior longitudinal ligament

The anterior longitudinal ligament runs along the anterior (front) surface of the vertebral bodies, stretching from the pelvic surface of the sacrum to the anterior tubercle of the atlas (C1) and the occipital bone of the skull. It helps to prevent hyperextension of the vertebral column.

What is the movment allowed at the hinge joints?

a. monaxial rotation movment

b. Monaxial gliding movement

c. Monaxial angular movement

d. Biaxial angular movement

c. Monaxial angular movement

Hinge joints, such as the elbow and knee, allow for monaxial angular movement, meaning movement in one axis. In the case of hinge joints, this is typically flexion (bending) and extension (straightening). They do not allow for rotation or gliding movements, making them monaxial joints.

Which ligament at the knee prevent tibia to slip forward?

a. Lateral collateral ligament

b. Posterior cruciate ligament

c. Patellar ligament

d.Anterior cruciate ligament

d. Anterior cruciate ligament

The anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) is one of the key ligaments that helps stabilize the knee joint. It prevents the tibia (shinbone) from slipping forward under the femur (thighbone). Injury to the ACL is a common and often serious sports injury that can lead to knee instability.

What part/s of the appendicular skeleton attached with the axial skeleton?

a. Clavicle

b. Thoracic ribs

c. Hip bone

d. Both clavicle and hip bone

d. Both clavicle and hip bone

The appendicular skeleton is connected to the axial skeleton at two primary locations:

1. The clavicles (collarbones) connect with the sternum (breastbone) of the axial skeleton at the sternoclavicular joints, forming part of the shoulder girdle.

2. The hip bones (also known as coxal bones or pelvic bones) connect with the sacrum of the axial skeleton at the sacroiliac joints, forming part of the pelvic girdle.

The thoracic ribs are actually a part of the axial skeleton, not the appendicular skeleton, as they attach to the thoracic vertebrae and the sternum.
what is this thing Which bone articulates with the parietal bones, the temporal bones, the sphenoid, and the first cervical?

The bone that articulates with the parietal bones, the temporal bones, the sphenoid, and the first cervical vertebra (the atlas) is the occipital bone. This bone forms the posterior and much of the base of the skull. The first cervical vertebra (the atlas) articulates with the occipital bone at the atlanto-occipital joint, allowing for the nodding motion of the head.
Which bone articulates with the parietal bones, the temporal bones, the sphenoid, and the first cervical?

The occipital bone articulates with the parietal and temporal bones, the sphenoid bone, and the first cervical vertebra (C1), which is also known as the atlas. This articulation with the atlas occurs at the atlanto-occipital joint, allowing for the nodding movement of the head. The lambdoid suture is where the occipital bone meets the parietal bones, and the occipitomastoid suture is where it meets the temporal bones. The occipital bone also forms a joint with the sphenoid bone towards the base of the skull, known as the spheno-occipital synchondrosis, which ossifies over time.
The carotid canal located in the_____?

The carotid canal is located in the petrous part of the temporal bone. This canal contains the internal carotid artery, which supplies blood to the brain. The canal begins at the base of the skull, just medial to the styloid process, and ascends to reach the middle cranial fossa, where the internal carotid artery enters the cranial cavity.
The foramen ovale located in which cranium bone?

The foramen ovale is located in the sphenoid bone of the cranium. It is one of the several openings (foramina) in the sphenoid bone and provides a passageway for the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V3) to exit the cranial cavity.
the Mental foramen located in which facial bone ?

The mental foramen is located in the mandible, which is the lower jawbone and considered a part of the facial bones. It is positioned on the anterolateral surface of the mandible, typically near the apex of the second premolar tooth. The mental foramen allows the passage of the mental nerve and blood vessels out of the mandibular canal to supply the chin and lower lip.
The supraorbital foramen located in which the cranium bone ?

The supraorbital foramen is located in the frontal bone of the cranium. It is situated just above the orbit (eye socket) and allows the passage of the supraorbital artery and nerve, which supply the forehead and scalp. In some individuals, this feature exists as a notch rather than a complete foramen.
What is the pelvic opening bounded by the coccyx, the ischial tuberosities, and the inferior border of the pubic symphysis called?

The pelvic opening bounded by the coccyx, the ischial tuberosities, and the inferior border of the pubic symphysis is called the pelvic outlet. The pelvic inlet and outlet define the superior and inferior openings of the pelvic cavity, respectively. The shape and size of the pelvic outlet are important anatomical features, especially during childbirth.
What are the significant features of the female ilium

The female pelvis, including the ilium, which is the largest bone of the hip, has several distinctive features that are generally adapted for childbirth. Some of the significant features of the female ilium include:

1. Broader, Shallower Structure: The female ilium tends to be more broad and shallow compared to that of males, which leads to a wider pelvic cavity.

2. Greater Flare: The iliac wings of the female pelvis flare out more than those of the male pelvis, further contributing to a broader pelvic inlet and a wider pelvic outlet.

3. Lower Iliac Crests: In females, the highest points of the iliac crests, which are the superior borders of the ilium, are often lower relative to the rest of the body, altering the overall shape of the pelvis.

4. More Forward Tilt: The female pelvis tends to tilt forward more, which changes the angle of the pelvic inlet.

5. Larger Pelvic Inlet and Outlet: As a result of the broader structure and increased flare, the pelvic inlet and outlet are larger, providing more room for delivering a baby.

It's important to note that there is significant variation among individuals, and not all women will have all these features to the same degree. Nonetheless, these generalizations reflect typical differences seen when comparing a female ilium to that of a male.
What is the lateral bone connected to the elbow joint at the capitulum of the humerus bone?

The lateral bone connected to the elbow joint at the capitulum of the humerus is the radius. The radius is one of the two long bones in the forearm, the other being the ulna. The head of the radius articulates with the capitulum of the humerus to form part of the elbow joint, allowing for the rotation of the forearm as well as bending and straightening of the elbow.
Which cranial bone forms the anteromedial floor of the cranium, the roof of the nasal cavity and part of the nasal septum and medial orbital wall?

The ethmoid bone is the cranial bone that forms the anteromedial floor of the cranium, the roof of the nasal cavity, part of the nasal septum, and the medial wall of the orbits. It is a complex bone with a central portion called the perpendicular plate, which contributes to the formation of the upper part of the nasal septum, separating the two nasal cavities. The cribriform plate, which is the horizontal part of the ethmoid bone, forms the roof of the nasal cavity and has tiny foramina that allow for the passage of olfactory nerve fibers. Additionally, the ethmoid bone includes the ethmoidal labyrinth, consisting of multiple small air cells that contribute to the ethmoid sinuses, and it supports the superior and middle nasal conchae, which are important structures within the nasal cavity.
what is the anterior medial fossa located on the inferior anterior portion of the humerus and?

The anterior medial fossa located on the inferior anterior portion of the humerus is the coronoid fossa. This shallow depression accommodates the coronoid process of the ulna when the elbow is flexed. Opposite to it on the posterior aspect of the humerus is the olecranon fossa, which houses the olecranon process of the ulna when the elbow is extended. These fossae are important for allowing full flexion and extension at the elbow joint.
